speaker: interviewer
text: Miriam Schneid-Ofseyer. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, we came to the time when he went to Israel. He had one day to go and see his mother, his sister, and his brother-in-law. This was the last time that he saw them. He didn't know of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. Oh, I remember now where they had the pension. In Szczyrk. Szczyrk, that's the name of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere not too far. In Poland. Not too far from Bielitz. And he took with him another copy of that book.

speaker: interviewer
text: The manuscript that was confiscated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The manuscript. He had another copy. And he took with him all the other photographs that he had. Filled up a suitcase, didn't take much more, and went to Israel with the hope that he will be able to make a living and bring his mother to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know anyone there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not know a soul. And he came there and, you know, for an art historian to find a job is very, very difficult. It took him years until he got a job. But in the meantime, he has to live, so there were periods when he was just hungry, didn't have enough food to eat. He was living in an attic or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was trying. So what did he do? He lectured, because he knew Hebrew, luckily. He lectured and he got pennies for each and every lecture. I think two lirot, something that was like nothing. Where did he lecture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the kibbutzim and in some other institutions. Wherever they invited him, he was delighted to go. And he built for himself a projector that was very heavy and very large, almost as large as the things that Tony is carrying with him. And when he had to go to a kibbutz, for example, and had to go into a bus, he couldn't put it in the bus. He had to put it on the- on the roof of the bus. In other words, to climb and to put it and to tie it. It was very hard, and he got almost nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: We should just clarify that Tony is in fact the cameraman who is filming this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And then he also started to write and get payments for whatever he wrote. And there was a publisher from Tel Aviv who invited him to write a book about art history, which was his line. And he wanted him just to sum up what is in the art history. But Otto was a very thorough man, and he wouldn't sum up. He would find his own findings and would investigate and research and so on. And that gentleman was paying him according to the number of pages that Otto wrote. And he was writing very slowly, because each and every letter had to be like a painting-- very, very clear. His handwriting is unbelievably nice and clear. And he got nothing. And then we met. It was 1945, around Pesach, Passover. And we fell in love. He wasn't sure that he has the right to marry me because of the difference in ages. But when he came for dinner to my mother's house and brought a huge bouquet of flowers that I still remember, my mother understood why I loved him. And she did not stand in our way. And a few months later, we married. I was then studying in the Mizrahi seminar in Jerusalem to become a teacher, so no income on my part and pennies on his part. And he lived in a rented room that I shared now with him. And it was very uncomfortable and very hard. I remember one episode that I divided all the money that came in to 30 days or 31 days, and that was the amount that we could spend on that day. So one day, I tell him, Otto, you know, we have just, uh, 10 grosz-- which is, I don't know, 50 cents. That's all that we have for today. And if we are going to the mensa, to the students' restaurant to eat, only one of us can go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, then you go. And I said, no, no. I'm not hungry at all. You go. And we started to argue who is going to have this only lunch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came a telephone from my mother saying, come for lunch to me. So we took the money, we went on the bus and we went to mother. Times were very, very hard. And he was continuing to write and to lecture until, um, until the War of Independence broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did Otto to find out about the fate of his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened a few years before. We married in '45. Yaakov was born in '46, Erev Yom Kippur, one day before Yom Kippur. And this happened before Yaakov was born, so probably beginning of '46. Somebody sent him a letter, a person by the name of Dr. Bettor. And Dr. Bettor was in the Holocaust. And he saw that some members of his family were taken on that date, and he saw also his mother and sister and brother-in-law. They were sent to Auschwitz on Tisha B'Av, 1943. And from there, right to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Otto found this out--

speaker: interviewee
text: When Otto found this out, I came from the seminar. And I saw him sitting on the floor. I was always looking through the window when I would come.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I entered. What happened? Wow, he was in such misery. I never saw him like that. And he told me that he heard this, what happened, that his family's gone. There is nobody there left. And he's lost. What should he do? But here is a little baby, already growing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then a change came in his life, because he was-- since I believe '39, he became very, very religious. This was my luck, because my mother wouldn't allow me to marry a person who is not religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were brought up quite orthodox.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was brought up quite orthodox. My father belonged to the Lubavitch. And I still remember the songs that he would sing, you know, the Hasidic songs.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was praying three times a day. And when he was praying, he looked like a saint with the tallit and tefellin. He told me then, perhaps you will be upset, he said, but I decided that Judaism is the greatest, uh, belief in the world, but I don't believe anymore in prayers. I cannot understand why God didn't listen to me. I was praying with all my heart.  I was praying so hard and he didn't listen. So what is the sense saying, God, how good you are? Is he really that good? And he dropped the fulfillment of the prayers, of going to the synagogue. He still stayed a very-- a very faithful man to the Jewish people, like we have here in one of the songs. Shall I read  it? Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'To the loved ones. My heart goes out to my people, my people Israel, to the nation in its tragedy, to the nation in the fullness of its truth and goodness, to the redeeming people deserving a redeemer.' Concern of Israel and Judaism was number one in his life. The Holocaust was actually number one plus. He lived through the Holocaust. He didn't let him for a minute free. He was all the time talking and thinking about the Holocaust. And then he decided that he's going to erect a monument to the 6 millions, but about this we'll speak a bit-- a little later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he look at the manuscript that he had brought with him to Palestine, Israel at that time and reevaluate what he had in his hands?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He said that this manuscript that he has in his hand became a yizkor book, a book of remembrance, to many people who are not alive anymore. And because he lives in Israel and the language is Hebrew, he decided that he's going to be-- to do another heroic job. He will translate the book from German into Hebrew and update it right to all of the families of these artists to find out what happened with them during the Holocaust. And that's what he did. Ah, it took him quite a few years, until in 1959 he-- or actually, before that he found a publisher, a very important publisher in Israel. And he signed a contract with that publisher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the publisher?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't like to mention it because it's-- eh, OK. So he gave them the manuscript. And they went over it and they signed a contract with him. And then one day, he took a suitcase full with pictures and we went to Tel Aviv. We lived then in Yerushalayim. He went to Tel Aviv to go to the publisher and to give him the photos for the book that is going to be printed soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sitting and waiting for him in a coffee shop downstairs. And he said, I'll be there five minutes. And he came after five minutes, and he was pale like the wall. What did he say? They decided not to publish my book. So that is a manuscript that was almost published twice. What was the reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? What was the reason? They told him that the book by Cecil Roth about Jewish art was published just now and they cannot flood the market-- the market in Israel is not that huge-- with another book about Jewish art.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I said to him, you know what? I'll go to America. Perhaps I'll find a publisher. And this was when the idea came to me the first time. And in 1960, I left. Now, we're going to come back and talk about the treasure as it's been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --come to be called right now. But in the meantime, let's talk a bit about Otto's projects, his books, his things that were published, the exhibitions, and also some of the books that you have written that have been published during  this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, but perhaps if I may, before that I think that I should mention that in the meantime he got the job in the Technion in Haifa. And he was lecturing on the history of art and history of architecture. And we were making a nice living. I was teaching and he was lecturing in the Technion. So there was no problem, financial problem. And then I can answer your question about his books. The first book that he wrote I mentioned. It is in German, [GERMAN], that we are going to see perhaps later. It was published in three cities at the same time. And then he wrote [HEBREW], in Hebrew. And then he wrote [HEBREW], the in Hebrew. And it was also translated into English and it was a bestseller for bar mitzvah, bat mitzvah, I don't know what. He himself got a huge payment for this Rembrandt book, 15 lirot, which was nothing. So you can see that some of the publishers are taking good advantage of people who are poor and are happy to get anything, whatever comes. And then he wrote [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], And years later when we were in Toronto, he wrote And the last book was [GERMAN], poems in German, the late poems, the late poems that he wrote. He was also a dramatist. He wrote plays and a libretto to an opera and one-act plays. He was very prolific, I think like his great-grandfather Scheer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you also-- there's a book that has been published called which is a book-- can you tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. After Otto's death in 1974, I collected all the love poems that I wrote to him and all the love poems that he wrote to me and brought it to a publisher in Israel, to Eichid. And they-- and also drawings to illustrate, drawings by Otto. And it was published. And in this book in the preface, I am telling about Otto's last hours, actually it was the last few days, but the last time that he spoke to me in the hospital in Mount Sinai here in Toronto. They were bringing the breathing machine to connect him, and I was in the room, so they asked me to go. I cannot stay while they are doing that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I kissed him and I went. When I was near the door, he stopped me. And he said, breathe, breathe. I said, do I have to breathe? It's you who has to breathe. The breathing machine is going to be put on him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no. You. Which is very touching to me, because he knew that he's going to die and he didn't think about himself. He was thinking about me. And he was afraid that after his death I will not be able to live. So actually, what he said to me was, live, continue living. All his sickness lasted 36 days. It was a very, very speedy cancer of, uh, of the lymphs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to go back a bit, though, can we talk about your sons and your life and also a bit about you as a poet?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Uh, we have two sons. Yaakov was born in 1946 and Moshe Adam was born in 1950. Both of them darling children, as children and also now as adults. They are wonderful and very devoted. There was a great love between Otto and the children. He would call, for example, Yaakov, Yaakov libi, Yaakov my heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the children grew up nicely, until in 1956 Otto decided that he wants to write another book about art of Greek people. One book was not enough, so he wants to go to Greece. And we decided to take with us also Yaakov. Oh, a little child, 10 years old, what is he going to understand? Nothing, but let's take him. He will not disturb us. So we took him and we spent a month in Greece. And after that month when we came back home, Yaakov didn't stop to talk about Greece. He would tell about Greece to anybody who entered the house. And one day he said to me, ah, Ima, do you remember what happened in Delphi? And I said, yes, of course. I was there with you. 'I want to tell it to you.'

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you know, I'm busy now. I'm preparing lunch. Here is a piece of paper and a pencil, and write whatever you want. So for a while it was quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he said, Ima, I am tired. I cannot write anymore. My hand is painful. Why can't you-- he had the flu on those days. Why can't you sit near me and I will dictate to you? Half an hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: I sat near him for two weeks. And he was pouring all that accumulated. And everything was so fascinating. And I was writing, writing exactly what he told me. And then when it was finished, it was a heap of pages.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otto said, what are we going to do with it? I said, what  do you think? He said, put in a drawer. You have so many manuscripts, will be another one. I said, OK. But I would like to show it to one publisher. And if he will want it, then we'll decide. So I went to that publisher in Tel Aviv who publishes children's books. And he grabbed it. And it was a best seller in Israel. And the beauty of that book-- yeah. He divided it into two because it was so large. The first one is called [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and the other one [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH],

speaker: interviewee
text: And Yaakov made drawings for these books, very nice drawings, childish. But all the idea was that you see the world through the eyes of a child. And the world looks completely different.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was he again?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10. When it was published, it was- he was 10. He was probably 9 1/2 when he wrote it. But-- eh, I'm sorry about that, but I cannot change it-- there were some people who wrote very nicely about him. Yaakov Schneid [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], Yaakov Schneid, the genius 10 years old, a full page in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were some people who were suspicious. You never saw such a young writer. How do we know that he wrote the book? Perhaps his mother who is a writer and his father who is a writer, perhaps they wrote the book for him. He was so offended that afterwards he didn't want to publish anything. He wrote other books, but he simply didn't allow me to give them to any publisher, till one newspaper wrote to us a letter saying, because there are question marks, would you allow us to take Yaakov to somewhere-- it didn't say where-- and let him write? And we want to make sure that he knows how to write.

speaker: interviewee
text: We said, OK. Take him. So they took him to Tel Aviv. They brought him to the zoo. And then they took him to the editorial room. They closed the door. Sit and write.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did he write? He saw the lion. Poor lion, walking back and forth. He wants the forest. He wants the jungles. He wants to be free. Why are you closing him, and so on? Exactly the same style that he wrote [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. So those people when they saw it said, this child who wrote in our presence these lines, he's the one who wrote [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. So they took away, you know, the mud that was thrown against him. And-- but he, you see, he never published anything afterwards. And today what does he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today he's a-- first of all, he's a psychologist. And he's a consultant to companies regarding money and all that. Not exactly fitting with his character, but he's very good at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your other son.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other son, Moshe Adam, we called him cheeky. Why cheeky? Because instead of saying Moshe Adam, we said Moshik. And when you ask them, what is your name, he said 'shik. So it became cheeky. He's a psychologist today. And he lives in Vancouver and works for the government. And he has a wife and a baby that was born many years after their marriage, but adorable.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll see photographs of them later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me also about the other poem that was written by Otto--

speaker: interviewee
text: By Otto?

speaker: interviewer
text: To you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Love poem?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. 'A blessed hour. Your language is my language. 20 years and 1 in this language of ours we conversed and were silent and heart to heart did ask and also answered. Everything is ours because we belong to everything. Into a great covenant we entered at the time of grace. And behold, it is still with us. I you, and you me, did create at the blessed hour.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you also tell us a bit about your accomplishments as a poet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Again, it started with Otto. One day he came home and he said, you know that there is a competition by ACUM, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which is the association of composers and writers in Israel, and I think that you should participate. So I asked him, what are the rules? Oh, he can get it from this and this. He got the rules. And the rules were that you have to-- you cannot sign your name. It's an anonymous manuscript that you are sending them. And in a special envelope, you write-- you have a pen name. You write Tamar is Miriam Schneider.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided to send. And after a while, I get a letter. Can you come to our offices in Tel Aviv? I didn't know why. But when I came, Avidom, who was the head of the Association of Composers and Writers, stood up and said, lady, you are the winner of the first prize for poetry. This was really a surprise. I didn't expect anything like that. So that was the first book that-- no, actually the second. One was in prose that was already published, a novel in letters. And the second one [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], And it was published afterwards by [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which was a very distinguished publishing house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, uh, I-- I went to Rubin Mass and Rubin Mass published the third, and so on and so forth. And then the rest of the books were published by Echid and Trachin. And all of them are eight books in Hebrew and two books in English. I started to write English only two years, three years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the exhibitions of Otto Schneid? Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Otto Schneid had exhibition-- it started with Firenze, Florence. When he had his exhibition in Florence, the Professor Poggi from the Galleria del Uffizi came to the exhibition. This Professor Poggi is the one, if you remember, that the Mona Lisa was stolen and somebody found her, this was Professor Poggi.

speaker: interviewee
text: But now he was very, very old. And he looked at the sculptures and the paintings and he just loved them. And he looked at the sculpture of that bust and he said, could we get it for the, uh, for the Galleria del Uffizi? So of course, Galleria del Uffizi is one of the best places in the world. But probably after a year, we got from him a letter saying that this is an outstanding sculpture, but a modern one. And the Uffizi doesn't have modern things. If we allow him to put it into the Pitti Palace? So we allowed him, and that's why it is in the Pitti Palace.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he had exhibitions in New York and in Scranton, Pennsylvania, and in Toronto. Wherever he went, there were exhibitions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were books written about him as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is one book that is called but you're going to show it afterwards. And there is a little booklet about his monument to the 6 millions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the end of Otto's life, what that entailed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The end of his life was that he decided that he has to return to writing. So by the beginning of the '70s, he started to write a philosophical book, first of all the poems in German that I mentioned before, and then he wrote a philosophical book, covering the whole area. He wrote about 400 pages, but he didn't finish it. After his death, I wanted it to be at least typed, because he was writing all kinds of notes here and there. I found a lady, unbelievable lady. Unfortunately, I forgot her name. She sat months and typed the whole manuscript. So I have it typed, but that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from the time he was diagnosed until the time he actually passed away, what were the conversations? What did he do during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before that he said that he thinks there is a problem with his thyroid gland. And he went to-- and he wrote to a friend in Israel who was a doctor and also a vegetarian. And he asked him what-- how can he cure himself. He's sure that he has something in the thyroid. But in the meantime, we went to a doctor here. And the doctor did the tests and said there is nothing in your thyroid, no problem. And I have the second letter that he wrote to his friend, the doctor in Israeli, Dr.  Aron Weiss. And he wrote to him, there is nothing to worry. I don't have any-- any problem with my, uh-- with my, uh, thyroid. And the cancer started in the thyroid a very short while later. So the test was not good. And he did not know that he's going to die. We promised him that he's going to live. But when they did the autopsy, the doctor told me that there is no chance. It took only 36 days. And he wasn't talking a lot. One day, I took him home for the weekend. And we were sitting in the garden and talking a little there. He didn't want to return to the hospital. But I couldn't-- I couldn't keep him at home, because I didn't have anything to help him.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to stop here and go to the next tape.

words.text: We're going to have to stop

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, OK.
